section.title: The Way, the Truth, and the Life
section.sectionRationale: With His departure looming, Jesus comforts His troubled disciples. He is going to prepare a place in the Father's house and will come again for them. To Thomas He gives the sixth I am saying, I am the way and the truth and the life, and to Philip He reveals that to see Him is to see the Father. He promises the coming of the Spirit, the Advocate, and leaves His peace, so that even His going is for their good.
section.thesis: Jesus calms His disciples' fear by promising to prepare a place for them and to come again, revealing Himself as the only way to the Father and the perfect image of the Father, and pledging the gift of the Spirit and His own peace, so that His departure is not abandonment but the path to a deeper union with the Father and the Son.
section.frameNote: Four movements: comfort in the promise of the Father's house and Jesus' return; the way, the truth, and the life, and the vision of the Father in the Son; the promise of the Spirit and the indwelling of the Father and the Son; and the gift of peace against a troubled heart.

units.label: The Father's house
units.note: I go to prepare a place for you

verses.text: “Do not let your hearts be troubled. You believe in God; believe in Me as well.
In My Father’s house are many rooms. If it were not so, would I have told you that I am going there to prepare a place for you?
And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come back and welcome you into My presence, so that you also may be where I am.
You know the way to the place where I am going.”

units.label: The way, the truth, and the life
units.note: anyone who has seen Me has seen the Father

verses.text: “Lord,” said Thomas, “we do not know where You are going, so how can we know the way?”
Jesus answered, “{{egoeimi|I am}} the {{hodos|way}} and the {{aletheia|truth}} and the {{zoe|life}}. No one comes to the Father except through Me.
If you had known Me, you would know My Father as well. From now on you do know Him and have seen Him.”
Philip said to Him, “Lord, show us the Father, and that will be enough for us.”
Jesus replied, “Philip, I have been with you all this time, and still you do not know Me? Anyone who has seen Me has seen the Father. How can you say, ‘Show us the Father’?
Do you not believe that I am in the Father and the Father is in Me? The words I say to you, I do not speak on My own. Instead, it is the Father dwelling in Me, performing His works.
Believe Me that I am in the Father and the Father is in Me, or at least believe on account of the works themselves.
Truly, truly, I tell you, whoever believes in Me will also do the works that I am doing. He will do even greater things than these, because I am going to the Father.
And I will do whatever you ask in My name, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son.
If you ask Me for anything in My name, I will do it.

units.label: The promise of the Spirit
units.note: another Advocate to be with you forever

verses.text: If you love Me, you will keep My commandments.
And I will ask the Father, and He will give you another {{parakletos|Advocate}} to be with you forever,
the Spirit of truth. The world cannot receive Him, because it neither sees Him nor knows Him. But you do know Him, for He abides with you and will be in you.
I will not leave you as orphans; I will come to you.
In a little while the world will see Me no more, but you will see Me. Because I live, you also will live.
On that day you will know that I am in My Father, and you are in Me, and I am in you.
Whoever has My commandments and keeps them is the one who loves Me. The one who loves Me will be loved by My Father, and I will love him and reveal Myself to him.”
Judas (not Iscariot) asked Him, “Lord, why are You going to reveal Yourself to us and not to the world?”
Jesus replied, “If anyone loves Me, he will keep My word. My Father will love him, and We will come to him and make Our home with him.
Whoever does not love Me does not keep My words. The word that you hear is not My own, but it is from the Father who sent Me.
All this I have spoken to you while I am still with you.
But the Advocate, the Holy Spirit, whom the Father will send in My name, will teach you all things and will remind you of everything I have told you.
Peace I leave with you; My peace I give to you. I do not give to you as the world gives. Do not let your hearts be troubled; do not be afraid.
You heard Me say, ‘I am going away, and I am coming back to you.’ If you loved Me, you would rejoice that I am going to the Father, because the Father is greater than I.
And now I have told you before it happens, so that when it does happen, you will believe.
I will not speak with you much longer, for the prince of this world is coming, and he has no claim on Me.
But I do exactly what the Father has commanded Me, so that the world may know that I love the Father. Get up! Let us go on from here.

egoeimi.gloss: I am. In the sixth great I am saying Jesus declares, I am the way and the truth and the life. As with the other sayings, the phrase names both a rich reality and the divine self. Jesus is not merely a signpost to the Father but the way itself, and no one comes to the Father except through Him.

hodos.gloss: Way, road, path. To Thomas's question about the way, Jesus answers that He is the way, the sole road to the Father. Early believers were even called followers of the Way. The truth and the life are bound to it: He is the way because He is the truth revealed and the life given, so that coming to the Father runs only through Him.

aletheia.gloss: Truth, reality as God defines it. Jesus is the truth in person, the full and reliable revelation of God, so that to know Him is to know the Father. His truth is not abstract doctrine but a person to be trusted and followed, the one in whom the Father is truly seen.

zoe.gloss: Life, the life of God, eternal life. Jesus is the life, its source and giver; because He lives, His people will live. The way, the truth, and the life together declare that Jesus is the only path to the Father, the true revelation of Him, and the life that union with Him brings.

parakletos.gloss: Advocate, Comforter, Helper, Counselor, one called alongside. Jesus promises another Advocate, the Holy Spirit, of the same kind as Himself, to be with the disciples forever. The Spirit continues Jesus' presence with them, teaching, reminding, and dwelling within, so that His departure gives way to an even closer indwelling of God.

basic.context: The disciples are shaken by Jesus' talk of leaving. He tells them not to be troubled: He is going to His Father's house to prepare a place for them, and He will come back for them. When Thomas asks how they can know the way, Jesus says, I am the way and the truth and the life; no one comes to the Father except through Me. When Philip asks to see the Father, Jesus says that to see Him is to see the Father. He then promises the Holy Spirit, another Advocate who will be with them forever and live in them, and He leaves them His peace, so their hearts need not be afraid.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter is pastoral comfort grounded in who Jesus is. The key questions are, how can we come to the Father, and how will we cope when Jesus is gone. Jesus answers that He alone is the way to the Father, that seeing Him is seeing the Father, and that His going will bring the Spirit and a deeper indwelling of God. The exclusivity of verse 6 is not narrow bigotry but the good news that the only way to God has been opened for all in Christ. Love for Jesus shows itself in keeping His word.
basic.originalLanguages: The heart of the chapter is the sixth I am saying: ego eimi, I am the hodos, the way, and the aletheia, the truth, and the zoe, the life. And Jesus promises another parakletos, Advocate, the Spirit who will be with and in His people forever.
basic.typology: The Father's house with many rooms fulfills the longing for God's dwelling with His people. The way to the Father, once guarded since Eden, is opened in Christ. The promised Spirit fulfills the prophets' hope of God's Spirit poured out and dwelling within.
basic.christ: Christ is the way, the truth, and the life, the only access to the Father and His perfect image. He is one with the Father, dwelling in Him and He in the Son. He goes to prepare a place, sends the Spirit, and gives a peace the world cannot give.
basic.god: God is the Father whose house has room for many, who is seen in the Son, who sends the Spirit in the Son's name, and who, with the Son, comes to make His home in those who love Him. The triune God is unveiled in this chapter.
basic.crossReferences: John 1 said the Son makes the Father known. Ephesians 2 speaks of access to the Father through Christ. Romans 8 describes the Spirit's indwelling. Ezekiel 36 promised God's Spirit within His people.
basic.oneStory: The way back to the Father, closed since the garden, is reopened in Jesus, the way, the truth, and the life. He goes ahead to prepare a place and sends the Spirit so that the triune God comes to dwell with His people, bringing a peace the troubled world cannot give and a hope of being forever where He is.

context.literary: The second major movement of the Upper Room Discourse, marked by questions from Thomas, Philip, and Judas (not Iscariot) that draw out Jesus' teaching. It moves from comfort about His departure, to the sixth I am saying, to the first of the Paraclete promises, to the gift of peace. The theme of mutual indwelling, the Son in the Father, believers in the Son, the Spirit in believers, runs throughout, and the chapter ends with a call to rise and go.
context.historical: In a culture where a departing head of household prepared for the family's future, the image of preparing a place resonates deeply. The word Paraclete described one called alongside to help, including in a legal sense as advocate. The saying the Father is greater than I reflects the Son's role in His mission and incarnation, not inequality of nature, and was much discussed in the early church's articulation of the Trinity.
context.canonical: I am the way, the truth, and the life stands with the New Testament's insistence that access to God is through Christ alone, Acts 4:12, 1 Timothy 2:5, Hebrews 10:19-20. The Paraclete promises unfold across chapters 14 to 16 and are fulfilled at Pentecost, Acts 2. The indwelling of the Father, Son, and Spirit grounds later Trinitarian theology, and the peace Jesus gives fulfills the prophetic shalom of the messianic age, Isaiah 9:6-7.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To comfort the disciples in the face of Jesus' departure by revealing Him as the only way to the Father and the Father's perfect image, and by promising the Spirit, the indwelling of God, and His peace, so that their hearts need not be troubled.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The comfort and promises are given first to the eleven, yet they extend to all who believe: Jesus remains the only way to the Father, the Spirit indwells all His people, and His peace is offered to every troubled heart. The call to show love by keeping His word is binding on all disciples.

debates.question: Is I am the way, the truth, and the life narrowly exclusive?

views.label: Exclusive and good news
views.case: Jesus plainly says no one comes to the Father except through Him; this is not arrogance but the announcement that the one way to God has been graciously opened for all people in Christ.

views.label: Exclusive in Christ, universal in scope
views.case: The exclusivity of the way is matched by the breadth of the invitation, for the way is offered to everyone; the same Gospel that says only through Christ also says the door is open to all who come.

debates.bearing: Both hold that salvation is through Christ alone and yet freely offered to the whole world, so the exclusivity is the ground of a universal welcome, not a barrier to it.

debates.question: What does the Father is greater than I mean?

views.label: Greater in role within the mission
views.case: In His incarnate mission the Son submits to and is sent by the Father; the greatness refers to order and role, not to any inferiority of nature, since elsewhere Jesus claims equality with and oneness with the Father.

views.label: The disciples should rejoice at His homegoing
views.case: In context, the point is that the disciples should be glad Jesus returns to the Father's glory, from which He came, rather than clinging to His earthly presence.

debates.bearing: Read with the rest of John, which affirms the Son's deity and oneness with the Father, the saying speaks of the Son's submitted role in redemption, giving the disciples reason to rejoice in His return to the Father.

study.originalLanguages: At the center stands the sixth I am saying: ego eimi, I am the hodos, the way, and the aletheia, the truth, and the zoe, the life. Jesus is not merely a guide pointing to the Father but the very road to Him; He is the truth because He is the full and faithful revelation of God, so that to see Him is to see the Father; and He is the life, its source and giver, so that because He lives His people live. And He promises another parakletos, an Advocate of the same kind as Himself, the Spirit of truth, who will not merely visit but abide with the disciples and be in them forever, so that His going away opens the way to an even closer indwelling of the triune God.

typology.type: The Father's house and dwelling place
typology.fulfillment: The many rooms Jesus prepares, fulfilling the longing for God to dwell with His people, John 14:2-3 and Revelation 21:3.

typology.type: The way to God barred since Eden
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the way to the Father, reopening access to God, John 14:6 and Hebrews 10:19-20.
The Paraclete, the Spirit of truth, who abides with and in the disciples, John 14:16-17.

study.christ: Christ is the way, the truth, and the life, the exclusive yet freely offered access to the Father and the Father's perfect image, so that whoever has seen Him has seen the Father. He is one with the Father, dwelling in Him and the Father in Him, speaking the Father's words and doing His works. He goes ahead to prepare a place in the Father's house and pledges to come again for His own. He asks the Father to send another Advocate, and He Himself gives a peace the world cannot match. His departure, far from abandonment, is the doorway to the indwelling presence of God.
study.god: God is unveiled as Father, Son, and Spirit within this chapter. The Father's house has room for many; the Father is seen in the Son; the Father sends the Spirit in the Son's name; and the Father and the Son come together to make their home in those who love Jesus and keep His word. The triune God draws near, not merely alongside His people but within them by the Spirit. This is a God whose deepest purpose is loving communion, to have His people with Him where He is and to dwell in them now by His Spirit.

crossReferences.note: There is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved, echoing no one comes to the Father except through Me.
There is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus, the sole way to the Father.
The Spirit of God dwells in you, unfolding the promise that the Advocate will be in the disciples.
The Prince of Peace whose government brings endless peace, fulfilled in the peace Jesus gives.

study.oneStory: The way back to the Father, closed when humanity was driven from the garden, is reopened in Jesus, who is Himself the way, the truth, and the life. He goes ahead to prepare a place in the Father's house and sends the Spirit so that the triune God comes to make His home with His people, not merely beside them but within them. His departure is not loss but gain, opening the door to a deeper communion with God and a peace the troubled world can neither give nor take away, while His people wait to be forever where He is.

quiz.q: How does Jesus comfort His troubled disciples at the start of the chapter?

options.text: He promises to prepare a place for them in the Father's house and to come back for them.
options.note: Verses 1 to 3 ground their comfort in His preparing a place and returning.

options.text: He simply tells them not to worry, since worry changes nothing.
options.note: He does say let not your hearts be troubled, but the comfort rests on the prepared place and His return, not positive thinking.

options.text: He assures them He will never leave in the first place.
options.note: He is indeed going away, but promises to return and prepare a place.

options.text: He calls them to trust God and Him amid their fear.
options.note: True, He calls for faith, but the specific comfort is the promise of a prepared place and His return.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by I am the way, the truth, and the life?

options.text: He is Himself the only access to the Father, His true revelation, and the source of life.
options.note: Verse 6 makes Him the exclusive way, the truth, and the life, through whom alone we come to the Father.

options.text: He is one good path among many to God.
options.note: He says no one comes to the Father except through Him, not one path among many.

options.text: He points people toward the way, truth, and life.
options.note: He is not merely a signpost; He is the way, the truth, and the life in person.

options.text: He is the only way to the Father.
options.note: True, but it omits that He is also the truth and the life, the full claim of the verse.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say to Philip's request to see the Father?

options.text: Anyone who has seen Jesus has seen the Father, for He is in the Father and the Father in Him.
options.note: Verses 9 to 11 reveal that the Son perfectly displays the Father.

options.text: No one can ever know what the Father is like.
options.note: The opposite; the Father is truly seen in the Son.

options.text: The Father will be shown only after Jesus dies.
options.note: The Father is already seen in Jesus now; Philip has been looking at Him all along.

options.text: To know the Father, keep looking to Jesus.
options.note: True in direction, but it softens the striking claim that seeing Jesus is seeing the Father.

quiz.q: What does Jesus promise about the Advocate, the Holy Spirit?

options.text: The Father will give another Advocate to be with them forever and to live in them.
options.note: Verses 16 and 17 promise the abiding, indwelling Spirit.

options.text: The Spirit will come only briefly and then leave.
options.note: The Spirit is given to be with them forever, not briefly.

options.text: The Spirit will replace Jesus with a lesser presence.
options.note: The Spirit is another Advocate of the same kind, continuing Jesus' presence, not a lesser substitute.

options.text: The Spirit will teach them and remind them of Jesus' words.
options.note: True, verse 26, but it omits the central promise that the Spirit will be with and in them forever.

quiz.q: What kind of peace does Jesus leave with His disciples?

options.text: His own peace, not as the world gives, so their hearts need not be troubled or afraid.
options.note: Verse 27 contrasts His peace with the world's and calms their fear.

options.text: A calm that comes from their circumstances improving.
options.note: His peace does not depend on easier circumstances; He gives it even as trouble remains, unlike the world's peace.

options.text: The same fragile peace the world offers.
options.note: He explicitly says He does not give as the world gives.

options.text: A peace that steadies them against fear.
options.note: True, but it omits that it is His own peace, unlike anything the world can give.

questions: Jesus met troubled hearts with the promise of a prepared place. When your heart is troubled, where do you turn for comfort, and how might His promise steady you?
Jesus claims to be the only way to the Father. How do you respond to that exclusivity, and how is it actually good news for a world seeking God?
To see Jesus is to see the Father. When you picture God, how closely does that picture match the Jesus of the Gospels?
Jesus said love for Him shows itself in keeping His word. Where is your love for Christ being tested by a command you find hard to obey?
Jesus gives a peace unlike the world's. In what situation do you most need to receive His peace rather than the fragile calm the world offers?

translationNotes.note: Many rooms; the BSB footnote offers an alternate punctuation of the verse. The image is of ample room in the Father's house for all Jesus' people.
Advocate renders parakletos; the BSB footnote gives Comforter, Helper, or Counselor. The word means one called alongside to help, and applies to the Spirit here and in verse 26.
He abides with you and will be in you; the BSB footnote notes a variant reading is in you. The promise moves from the Spirit's present company to His future indwelling.
The Father is greater than I speaks of the Son's submitted role in His mission, not inferiority of nature, since John elsewhere affirms His oneness with the Father.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 14:1-31. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped. Em dashes in verses 11 (in Me, or at least believe) and 16 (with you forever) were converted to commas. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
